items.title: Drive access from a role-permission matrix per doctype and action
items.description: Authorisation must be expressed as RolePermission rows that grant a role a specific action (read, create, submit, approve, cancel) on a specific doctype. The application checks this matrix before every protected operation, so permissions are data-driven and auditable rather than hard-coded into controllers.
items.applicability: Every protected document operation in a generated ERP with multiple roles.
items.example: A RolePermission row {role: AccountsClerk, doctype: JournalEntry, action: create} lets a clerk create journals; the absence of an approve row for that role blocks approval at the service boundary.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the check in a central authorisation service called by every operation, not per controller, so a missing check cannot slip through. Test implication: assert an action with no matching RolePermission is denied. The matrix governs which action a role may take; it does not by itself scope which company's rows the action touches, which is a separate concern.

items.title: Enforce maker-cannot-approve with approval thresholds
items.description: The user who creates or submits a document (the maker) must not be able to approve it (the checker), and approval may additionally require a threshold — a higher role or second approver above a value limit. This segregation of duties is a core internal control against unilateral action and fraud.
items.applicability: Approval workflows of any generated ERP handling financial or sensitive documents.
items.example: A clerk submits a 50,000 payment; the system rejects their own approval and routes it to a manager because the amount exceeds the clerk-tier threshold.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compare the approver identity to the recorded maker and to the amount threshold inside the approval service so the rule cannot be bypassed by a direct call. Test implication: assert the maker is refused approval of their own document and that an over-threshold document needs the higher role. Segregation of duties assumes distinct user accounts; shared logins defeat it, so account discipline is a precondition the control cannot enforce.

items.title: Require a reason on rejection
items.description: When a checker rejects a document, a non-empty reason must be captured and stored with the rejection. This makes the control auditable, gives the maker actionable feedback, and prevents silent or unexplained rejections from obscuring the approval trail.
items.applicability: The reject path of every approval workflow in a generated ERP.
items.example: A manager rejects an expense claim and must enter 'missing receipt'; the reason is persisted on the document history and surfaced to the submitter.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate the reason server-side as required so a blank rejection is refused. Test implication: assert a reject call with no reason is rejected and that a valid reason is persisted on the audit trail. The system enforces that a reason exists, not that it is accurate or meaningful; quality of the reason remains a human-process matter.

items.title: Keep the audit log append-only
items.description: Audit rows are insert-only: no application path may update or delete an existing audit record. The trail records what happened and must remain a faithful, tamper-evident history; corrections are made by appending new entries, never by rewriting old ones.
items.applicability: The audit subsystem of any generated ERP with compliance or internal-control needs.
items.example: An erroneous status change is corrected by appending a compensating audit entry; the original entry is left intact so the full sequence of events is preserved.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: expose only an insert API for audit and grant the application no update/delete path to the audit table. Test implication: attempt to edit or delete an audit row through the application and assert it is impossible. Application-level append-only does not stop a privileged DBA with direct table access; true tamper-evidence requires database-level controls or hashing beyond the application's reach.

items.title: Stamp every audit row with actor, UTC time and entity
items.description: Each audit entry must record who acted (actor), when in UTC, and which entity and action were affected. UTC avoids timezone ambiguity across deployments, and the actor-plus-entity stamp makes the trail answerable to 'who did what to which record, and when'.
items.applicability: Every audited operation in a generated ERP.
items.example: Approving an invoice writes {actor: userId, utc: 2026-06-21T10:15:00Z, entity: SalesInvoice#123, action: approve}.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: capture the actor from the authenticated principal, not a client-supplied field, and always store UTC. Test implication: assert the audit row's actor matches the acting user and the time is UTC. The stamp identifies the application user; it does not by itself prove the human behind the account, which depends on the strength of authentication.

items.title: Scope data by company
items.description: Documents and master data carry a company key, and every query is scoped to the user's permitted company so one company's data is not visible to another in a multi-company deployment. The scope is applied in the data and service layer so it cannot be omitted by a forgetful caller.
items.applicability: All data access in a generated ERP that hosts more than one company.
items.example: A user in Company A listing invoices sees only CompanyId == A rows; a crafted request for a Company B invoice id returns not-found rather than the row.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply the company filter centrally (a query filter or enforced service parameter) rather than per query. Test implication: assert a user cannot read or act on another company's document even by guessing its id. Company scoping is enforced in queries and services, not as row-level database security; see the dedicated boundary item for what that does not protect against.

items.title: Enforce unique business keys at the model
items.description: Natural business identifiers (document number per company, account number per company, item code) must be enforced as unique constraints at the model and database level, not merely checked in application code. A database unique index is the only guarantee that survives concurrent inserts and direct data access.
items.applicability: Entities with a natural business key in a generated ERP.
items.example: A composite unique index on (CompanyId, InvoiceNumber) prevents two invoices sharing a number within a company even under concurrent submission.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: declare the unique index in EF model configuration so it is created by migration, and handle the resulting unique-violation gracefully. Test implication: attempt a duplicate key under concurrency and assert exactly one insert succeeds. A unique constraint guarantees distinctness, not correct numbering; gap-free sequential numbering, if required, needs a separate generation strategy.

items.title: Add a RowVersion concurrency token to documents on SQL Server
items.description: Documents must carry a RowVersion (rowversion/timestamp) concurrency token so EF Core detects concurrent edits: if two users load the same document and both save, the second save fails an optimistic-concurrency check rather than silently overwriting the first. This prevents lost updates on shared documents.
items.applicability: Editable or state-transitioning documents in a generated ERP on SQL Server.
items.example: Two approvers open the same journal; the first save succeeds, the second is rejected with a concurrency exception prompting a reload, so the first user's change is not lost.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: map the RowVersion as a concurrency token in EF and catch DbUpdateConcurrencyException to surface a reload prompt. Test implication: simulate two stale saves and assert the second is rejected. RowVersion is a SQL Server-specific type; portable SQLite tests must use an alternative concurrency mechanism or skip this assertion.

items.title: Test RBAC-negative, maker/checker-negative and audit immutability
items.description: The generator should emit negative-path tests that prove the controls hold: a user without the required RolePermission is denied, a maker cannot approve their own document, and an attempt to mutate an audit row fails. Negative tests are what demonstrate a control actually blocks the disallowed action rather than merely permitting the allowed one.
items.applicability: CI coverage of the access-control and audit subsystems in a generated ERP.
items.example: Tests assert that a roleless user's create call returns forbidden, that the maker's approve call on their own document is rejected, and that an audit-row update throws.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: write the negative cases against the same service boundary real callers use, not a bypassed internal method. Test implication: each control gets an explicit denial test, not only a happy-path test. Passing negative tests shows the implemented controls block the tested actions; they cannot prove the absence of every conceivable bypass, which needs adversarial review.

items.title: Treat company isolation as service-level, not row-level database security
items.description: Company isolation is enforced in the application's queries and services, which is sufficient to keep one company's users from seeing another's data through the application. It is not row-level security in the database, so a determined DBA with direct database access can still read all companies' rows. This boundary must be stated honestly.
items.applicability: Threat-modelling and integration planning for multi-company deployments of a generated ERP.
items.example: Documentation notes 'company scoping is enforced in services/queries; it is not DB row-level security' so an operator knows direct SQL access bypasses it and plans DBA access controls accordingly.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the service-level filter rigorous and centralised, and document the boundary rather than implying database-enforced isolation. Test implication: assert application paths isolate companies while acknowledging the test cannot cover direct DB access. Deployments needing hard isolation from privileged operators must add database row-level security or separate databases; the generated baseline does not provide it.
